title: Reading UK electrical wiring colour codes
subtitle: Identify line, neutral, and earth conductors in modern, harmonised, and older British wiring
excerpt: How to read the colour codes on UK twin-and-earth cable and flexible flex, including the modern brown-blue-green-and-yellow standard, the harmonised 2004 change, and the older red-black-green system still found in pre-2004 installations. A reference article for any small electrical job in an older property.
sourceNotes: BS 7671:2018 (IET Wiring Regulations) reference. UK harmonisation of cable colours, 31 March 2004.

glossaryTerms.definition: The flat grey or white-sheathed cable used for fixed wiring in UK domestic installations. Carries one insulated line conductor, one insulated neutral conductor, and a bare earth conductor. Sized by core cross-section in square millimetres (1.0, 1.5, 2.5, 4.0, 6.0).

A round multi-strand cable used for appliance leads and lamp wiring. Contains stranded line, neutral, and earth conductors, each insulated. The standard appliance flex is 2 core (no earth) or 3 core (with earth) in 0.5, 0.75, 1.0, or 1.5 mm squared.

The European-aligned colour scheme adopted in the UK on 31 March 2004 for new electrical installations. Brown for line, blue for neutral, and green-and-yellow striped for earth. Replaced the earlier UK system of red, black, and green.

content.text: Cable colours tell the user which conductor is which. The colours changed in 2004 when the UK aligned with the European

. Any property wired before 2004 still uses the old colours unless the wiring has been replaced. Any property wired since 2004 uses the new ones. Most homes have both, because partial rewiring leaves a mix.
The flat grey or white sheath of
cable runs through walls and floors to sockets, switches, and lights. The outer sheath cuts open to show two insulated cores and a bare copper earth.
(sleeved in green-and-yellow at the terminations): earth.
(sleeved in green at the terminations): earth.
Note that black in old fixed wiring means neutral; black in modern flex means something else. Knowing which kind of cable is in front of you is the first read.
connects an appliance to its plug or to a fused spur. The conductors are stranded rather than solid. The same 2004 harmonisation applies.
Worked example: rewiring a pre-2004 lamp

An old table lamp has red, black, and plain green wires at the plug end. The new plug top calls for brown, blue, and green-and-yellow. The red goes to line, the black goes to neutral, the green goes to earth. The colours are different but the function is the same. This is also true at the bulb-holder end inside the lamp.
Worked example: extending a pre-2004 socket ring

An older twin-and-earth ring has red and black cores. A spur run in modern cable has brown and blue cores. At the terminal of the existing socket, the red (old line) and the brown (new line) both go to the same terminal. The black (old neutral) and the blue (new neutral) go to the same neutral terminal. A short label should be wrapped at the join point so a future installer can see the mix. The IET Regulations require the mix to be marked at any junction box, socket back, or switch box where the old and new colours meet.
A safety note on switch wiring

In a two-way light switch wired before 2004, one of the conductors carries a switched line (a switched live), not a neutral. A red cable in this position is line. A black cable in this position is also line; a black sleeve marked with red tape or a brown sleeve identifies the switched live. A reader who finds a black or blue conductor in a switch back should not assume it is neutral. A voltage tester confirms which conductor is live at any moment.

The procedure for replacing a socket, replacing a light fitting, or wiring a 13A plug all relies on this colour reference. The companion tutorials cover each step in detail; this article is the read-first reference.
